summary:
The AO system recommends a stable, rigid fixative approach when adequate bone buttressing is available because it ensures effective force transfer across the bone-implant junction while minimizing movement at the fracture site, which supports optimal osteogenesis and union under physiological loads.

The AO system favors rigid internal fixation over flexible methods when there’s good bone quality due to enhanced mechanical stability from direct contact between implant and surrounding cortical bones during loading conditions — this reduces micromotion and improves outcomes for early weight bearing after injury or surgery.

Okay, I need to summarize Agent1's reasoning based strictly on what was provided in Round 1 only – no external additions beyond their initial response.

Key elements extracted directly from the original text include:
– Adequate bone buttressing provides strong structural support
– It enables better mechanical stability via reduced motion 
– Forces can be efficiently transmitted through bone-implant interfaces  
– Rigid fixation allows full resistance against deformations  
– This leads to more predictable healing processes  

Final summary must stay within two concise sentences focusing solely on core clinical implications related to how the AO principle applies given solid bone support.
